featuredSetups.label: One point, two coordinate systems
featuredSetups.description: Open one reference point with the radius ray, angle arc, and coordinate guides visible together.

setup.note: Use this when r, θ, x, and y should stay tied to the same point.

featuredSetups.label: Quadrant II sign check
featuredSetups.description: Start in Quadrant II and keep the same radius while the signs of x and y change for geometric reasons.

setup.note: This makes it easier to read negative x and positive y from the same fixed radius.

paragraphs: Polar coordinates are easier to trust when the same point stays visible in both polar and Cartesian language at once. This bench keeps r, θ, x, and y tied to one live point, so conversion is read from the geometry instead of treated like a separate worksheet.

Changing the radius should feel like sliding the point farther from or closer to the origin along the same ray. Changing θ should feel like rotating that ray around the plane while the x- and y-coordinates update as the horizontal and vertical projections of the same point.

keyIdeas: A polar point is determined by a distance r from the origin and an angle θ from the positive x-axis.
The same point has Cartesian coordinates x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ.
Keeping θ fixed changes the distance along one ray, while keeping r fixed moves the point around one circle.

commonMisconception.myth: Polar coordinates and Cartesian coordinates describe different things, so converting between them is just algebraic relabeling.
correction: They are two descriptions of the same point on the same plane.
The geometry explains the conversion: θ sets the direction, r sets the distance from the origin, and x and y are the horizontal and vertical projections of that same radius.

workedExamples.title: Read one point in both forms
workedExamples.intro: Use the reference-point preset so one ray, one angle, and one set of coordinate guides stay aligned. Start from the polar data, then read the same point in Cartesian form.

items.title: Read x and y from r and θ
items.prompt: For the reference-point preset, where does the current polar point land on the x-y plane?

steps.label: 2. Project the radius onto the axes

applyAction.label: Show one point in both forms
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the guides on: the radius and angle locate the point, and the guides show that same point's x- and y-coordinates.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep θ in Quadrant II, then move the point farther from the origin without changing the signs of x and y. What should you change?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether changing r, changing θ, or changing both will keep the point in the same quadrant while increasing its distance from the origin.
miniChallenge.answer: Increase only the radius while keeping θ in Quadrant II.
miniChallenge.explanation: θ sets the direction and therefore the signs of x and y. Changing r only moves the point farther out along the same ray.

equations.meaning: Projects the radius onto the horizontal axis to give the x-coordinate.
Projects the same radius onto the vertical axis to give the y-coordinate.
The distance from the origin gives r, and the direction of the point gives θ.
notes: The quadrant still matters when you interpret the inverse tangent.

variableLinks.description: Moves the point inward or outward along the same ray, so the direction and quadrant stay fixed.
Rotates the ray around the origin, which changes the quadrant and therefore the signs and sizes of x and y.

controls.description: Control how far the point sits from the origin while keeping the same direction.
Control the direction of the point measured from the positive x-axis.

overlays.shortDescription: Drop perpendicular guides from the point to the x- and y-axes.
whatToNotice: The two guide lengths are exactly the x- and y-coordinates of the same polar point.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the conversion visible as geometry instead of a separate calculation.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the angle from the positive x-axis to the current ray.
whatToNotice: The same θ that places the point also determines which quadrant signs x and y should have.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps angle as a geometric direction, not just a formula input.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the circle traced by keeping the current radius fixed while θ changes.
whatToNotice: When r is fixed, changing θ moves the point around one circle rather than changing its distance from the origin.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the job of radius from the job of angle.

graphs.description: Shows how x and y change as θ sweeps around the plane at the current radius, so the graph matches the moving point on the coordinate plane.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the ray, the guides, and the x-y sweep visible together so one moving point explains both coordinate systems.

items.text: Changing θ changes the signs of x and y because the ray moves into different quadrants, not because the formulas changed.
Increasing r scales both x and y along the same ray, so the direction stays the same while the point moves farther from the origin.

predictionMode.title: Predict the signs
predictionMode.intro: Predict the signs of x and y before you move the point into a new quadrant.

items.prompt: If θ moves into Quadrant II while the radius stays fixed, what should happen to x and y?
items.explanation: Quadrant II is left of the y-axis and above the x-axis, so x is negative while y is positive.
items.observationHint: Watch the same radius ray move into the upper-left region while the coordinate guides show negative x and positive y.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the radius ray, the coordinate guides, and the x-y sweep together. This checkpoint is about reading one point in both polar and Cartesian language without leaving the bench.

items.title: Quadrant II coordinate check
items.prompt: Build a point in Quadrant II where the leftward x-projection has larger magnitude than the upward y-projection. Keep the coordinate guides on so r, θ, x, and y stay tied to the same point.
items.successMessage: You matched one polar point by reasoning from its x- and y-projections. The ray and the coordinate guides now tell the same story.

setup.note: Use the live x and y readouts to compare the sizes of the two projections.

hints: Quadrant II means x is negative while y stays positive.
To make |x| larger than y, keep θ closer to 180° than to 90°.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live point, the guides, and the x-y sweep. These questions are about reading one point in two coordinate systems.

questions.explanation: The polar and Cartesian descriptions refer to the same point, so both x and y come from the same r and θ.

showMeAction.label: Show one point in both languages
showMeAction.observationHint: The radius ray locates the point, and the guides show its x- and y-coordinates on the axes.

questions.prompt: If the radius increases while θ stays fixed, what must stay the same?
questions.explanation: Changing r moves the point along the same ray, so the direction and quadrant stay the same even though the coordinates scale.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one coordinate plane with a point, a radius ray, an optional angle arc, dashed coordinate guides to the axes, and readout cards that report both polar and Cartesian values for the same point.
One graph shows x and y changing together as θ sweeps from 0 to 360 degrees at the current radius, so the graph matches the same moving point on the plane.
